items.q: What is a black hole's event horizon?
items.correct: The boundary beyond which not even light can escape.
items.misconception: The visible 'edge' of the black hole.
items.random_wrong: A literal horizon you can walk to.
items.why: Students picture a surface. The event horizon is a mathematical BOUNDARY - nothing is physically there. Once inside, escape requires faster-than-light speed, which is impossible.

items.q: What does cosmological redshift tell us?
items.correct: Galaxies are receding from us due to the expansion of space.
items.misconception: Galaxies are turning red as they age.
items.random_wrong: Light gets tired and loses energy.
items.why: Students take redshift literally as colour change. It's a WAVELENGTH STRETCH - light waves get longer because space itself expands while light travels, shifting visible light toward the red end of the spectrum.

items.q: What is the principle behind stellar parallax?
items.correct: A nearby star appears to shift against background stars as Earth orbits the Sun.
items.misconception: Stars actually move position throughout the year.
items.why: Students think stars literally move. Parallax is an APPARENT shift due to our changing viewpoint - same reason your finger seems to jump when you alternate eyes. The closer the star, the bigger the shift.
Correct answer: Rs = 2GM/c^2. “Rs = GM/c” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Inside the event horizon, escape velocity exceeds:
items.why: It's Speed of light. “Sound speed” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Schwarzschild radius of a 1-solar-mass black hole in km (to nearest integer)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
